coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent gold nanocluster via polysaccharide-templated approach and its application for Cu2+ sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Ruizhi
Li, Hongchang
Chen, Bo
Wang, Liping
coredata.dc:description: In this report, we have constructed a simple, eco-friendly and one-pot synthetic strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters (GNCs) using a native polysaccharide (chondroitin sulfate) as the novel template. Moreover, we used some analytical instruments for characterizing the formation of Au clusters, containing ultraviolet-visible spectrometer, fluorescence spectrometer, high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The obtained data indicated that the Au nanoclusters were successfully formed. The chondroitin sulfate-capped gold nanoclusters (CS-GNCs) displayed excitation and emission bands at 450 and 520nm, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield was 4.78%. We further used CS-stabilized gold nanoclusters selectively and sensitively for sensing Cu2+ in real samples via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic bond. The linear range of the method was 0.01–1.8μM with the limit of detection (LOD) was 8nM (signal/noise=3), and the recoveries of the spiked samples in lake water and tap water samples ranged from 90% to 108%, suggesting that the proposed method could be used in the practice detection of Cu2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polysaccharide
Gold nanocluster
Template-based method
Cu2+ sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of CS-directed Au clusters
The study of stability of CS-directed gold nanoclusters
The linear curves of the fluorescence intensity of CS-protected Au nanoclusters vs. Cu2+
The selectivity of Cu2+ detection by CS-stabilized gold nanocluters
For Cu2+ sensing by CS-protected gold nanoclusters in real samples
Optimization of the synthetic conditions for CS-capped Au clusters
The spectra and structural characterization of as-prepared CS-AuNCs
Stability investigation of CS-encapsulated Au clusters
Cu2+ sensing using CS-AuNCs as fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CS-directed synthesis of fluorescent Au nanocluster and its application for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+.

Optimal conditions for preparing CS-directed Au nanoclusters. Effect of reducing agent (A), the molar concentration ratio of CS and HAuCl4 (B), reaction temperature (C) and reaction time (D) on the fluorescence intensity of CS-directed Au nanoclusters.

UV-vis absorption (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and fluorescence emission (blue line) of the as-prepared CS-AuNCs (A). The excitation wavelength was 450nm. Insert: the photographs of the aqueous solution of CS-AuNCs under visible (left), and UV light (right) with excitation at 365nm; XPS spectra of Au 4f for CS-templated Au nanoclusters (B); HRTEM images of the dilution of as-prepared fluorescent CS-Au clusters (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CS-AuNCs toward metal ions at physiological pH. Samples marked with 1–18 corresponding to blank, Cu2+, K+, Ag+, Na+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+, respectively. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of CS-AuNCs toward anions at physiological pH. Samples marked with 1–10 corresponding to blank, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −,SO4 2− and EDTA2−, respectively. The concentration of Cu2+ was 2.5μM, other concentration was 10μM. I0 and I are the PL intensities of the CS–AuNCs in the absence and presence of analytes, respectively. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

The fluorescence quenching curves of CS-AuNCs toward various conentration of Cu2+ (form top to bottom: 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.8, 2.5μM) (left); The quenched fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs in the presence of various concentration of Cu2+ (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.8, 2.5μM). Inset: The linear relationship of the quenching efficiency of CS–AuNCs vs. the added concentration of Cu2+ (0.01–1.8μM) (right). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
Detection of Cu2+ in lake water and tap water samples via the as-proposed CS-AuNCs (the water samples were diluted with 100-fold).

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of fluorescent gold nanocluster via polysaccharide-templated approach and its application for Cu2+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research, Ministry of Education, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Analysis and Testing Center, Changsha 410004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent gold nanoclusters were prepared by using chondroitin sulfate (CS) as the stabilizer via template-based strategy and their application for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic bond.

In this report, we have constructed a simple, eco-friendly and one-pot synthetic strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters (GNCs) using a native polysaccharide (chondroitin sulfate) as the novel template. Moreover, we used some analytical instruments for characterizing the formation of Au clusters, containing ultraviolet-visible spectrometer, fluorescence spectrometer, high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The obtained data indicated that the Au nanoclusters were successfully formed. The chondroitin sulfate-capped gold nanoclusters (CS-GNCs) displayed excitation and emission bands at 450 and 520nm, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield was 4.78%. We further used CS-stabilized gold nanoclusters selectively and sensitively for sensing Cu2+ in real samples via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic bond. The linear range of the method was 0.01–1.8μM with the limit of detection (LOD) was 8nM (signal/noise=3), and the recoveries of the spiked samples in lake water and tap water samples ranged from 90% to 108%, suggesting that the proposed method could be used in the practice detection of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, “magic number” sized noble metal nanoclusters (NMNCs) have emerged as a novel nanomaterials with broad applications especially in biosensing [1–4], bioimaging [5–7] and catalysis [8–10]. They fill up the miss link between atomic and nanoparticle behavior in metal [11]. Noble metal nanoclusters generally compose of a few to tens of metal atoms and their size are smaller than 2nm. Due to the size regime compared to wavelength of conduction electrons, the free electrons in metal nanoclusters are confined and the mean free path of the eletrons are shorter than the large nanoparticles, resulting in discrete and size-tunable electronic trasitions. On the basis of this merit, noble metal nanoclusters have been found to exhibit melecular-like propertied such as strong fluorescence [12–14]. Compared with semiconducter quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, noble metal nanoclusters revealing ultrafine size, high fluorescence, high stability in salt solution, non-tocicity and good biocompatibility, have gained more and more research interest. There are many proposed method for synthesizing noble metal nanoclusters, including alkanethiolate monolayer protected clusters (MPCs) [15–17], template-directed clusters [18–24], ligand-induced etching of metal nanoparticles [25,26], etc. Among many strategies for preparing noble metal nanoclusters, template-based method is high efficiency. Lots of macromolecules employed for templates contain polymers [19,20], polyelectrolytes [21], proteins [2,18], dendrimers [22,23], and DNA [24]. In order to expand the fundamental studies and application of fluorescent NCs in numerous fields, it is necessary for seeking novel marcomolecules for template-based methods.

In recent, increasing interest had been focused on the preparation and application of NCs in bioanalytical chemistry. Whereas, to the best of our knowledge, the preparation of NCs using polysaccharide as the template are scarce. Polysacchride, as natural biomacromolecule, possessing increased interest in numerous fields especially in drug delivery systems, is due to it is high stable, safe, non-toxic, good biocompatibility and biodegradable [27]. Moreover, it has low cost and abundant resources in nature, which are derived from animal origin (e.g., chondroitin, chitosan and glycogen), microbial origin (e.g., dextran, xanthan gum), plant origin (e.g., starch, cellulose and pectin) and algal origin (e.g., aliginate). More importantly, most of natural polysaccharides have hydrophilic functions, e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which can generate non-covalent bonds with biological tissues (mainly epithelia and mucous membranes), forming bioadhesion [28], which can provide a possibility path of treatment of diseases. Last but not the least, the above functional groups in polysaccharides can coordinate to metal cations such as Au3+. This behavior is similar to the biomineralization of organisms in nature [29,30]: sequestering and entrapping metal ions (e.g., Au3+, Ag+), accompanied by providing scaffolds for NCs generated. On the basis of this advantage, we hypothesize that they are possible for synthesizing NCs as the protective layer via template-directed approach.

To prove this hypothesis, a commercially available polysaccharide, chondroitin sulfate (CS), was chosen as the biomacromolecule template. CS, one of negative polysaccharides, is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan (GAG) consisted of a chain of alternating sugars (N-acetylgalactosamine and glucuronic acid) and usually found attached to proteins as part of a proteoglycan. In the past decades, CS was usually used to synthesize large nanoparticles and then apply for drug delivery systems [27]. Whereas, CS offers several crucial advantages as the template: (1) CS containing carboxyl and sulfonic groups can coordinate to metal cations; (2) CS as polysaccharide has good biocompatibility; (3) CS is mostly harmless because it is biodegradable. Therefore, we expect CS to be an ideal template for the preparation of NCs.

As we known, copper is an environmental pollutant with high toxicity and bioaccumulative properties [31]. While it also as the third essential transition metal element play a key role in human health [32], because it serves as a vital cofactor for many enzymes. If people over-intake Cu2+ derived from tap water or other matrials, the Cu2+ can accumulate in human body for a long time, leading to some diseases such as liver and kidney damage [33], cell damage of apoptosis [34,35] and central nervous system damage (e.g., Alzheimer’s disease and parkinson’s disease) [36,37]. So the U.S. Environmental Pretection Agency (EPA) defines the safe concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water at 1.3μg/mL (ca. 20μM). In current, due to the development of industry and the increase of people’s life, copper contamination is a serious proble needed to be resolved. Toward this goal, there are many traditional method for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+, e.g., atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (AAS or AES) and coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The above method owing to expensive instruments and/or complicated samples pretreatment have limited the applications of sensing Cu2+. Different from the above techniques, fluorescence-base probes (e.g., organic fluorophores) offer a facile and eco-effective approach for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in environmental and biological samples [38]. However, the organic probes often needed multi-steps organic reactions and complicated purification process have also limited the application of sensing Cu2+. To improve the preformance of sensing Cu2+ in real samples, accessible fluorescent probes need to be developed.

Herein, we reported a simple, cost-effective, and green strategy to synthesize water-soluble and highly fluorescent Au nanoclusters via template-based method by using commercially available polysaccharide termed CS as the template and dopamine (DA) as the reducing agent. Subsequently, we uesed the novel CS-AuNCs fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic bond (Scheme 1 ). The CS protective layer not only coordinated Cu2+ and reduced Cu2+ to Cu+, forming non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic bond; but also protected other interferences approaching the surface of gold nanoclusters. Based on the specific response, the as-prepared CS-AuNCs could be used for sensing Cu2+ in real samples.

All the chemicals and solvents were of at least analytical grade and commerically available. Chondroitin sulfate (CS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich chemical Co. (St. louis, MO). Dopamine (DA) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), ascorbic acid (AA), tetrachloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4∙4H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Water (>18.2MΩ) used for the experiments was purified by Milli-Q System. The solutions of Cu2+, K+, Ag+, Na+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+ were prepared from CuCl2, KCl, NaCl, Hg(NO3)2, MgCl2, Co(Ac)2, NiCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, CdCl2, Pb(Ac)2, Zn(Ac)2, Mn(Ac)2, CrCl3, AlCl3 and FeCl3, respectively. The aqueous solutions of F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −,SO4 2− and EDTA2− were prepared from NaF, NaCl, NaBr, KI, NaNO3, NaNO2, NaH2PO4, Na2SO4 and EDTA, respectively.

Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV light with excitation wavelength at 365nm was used. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan), the emission spectra were recorded using 10nm/10nm slit widths. The morphology of the CS-AuNCs was characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), HRTEM images were recorded with an high resolution electron microscope operated at 300kV (JEOL JEM-3100FEF). The oxidation state of Au clusters was obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the spectra were recorded with a K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, England) using a micro-focused, monochromatic Al Kα X-ray (72W, 400μm).

All glasswares used in the experiment were cleaned in a bath of freshly prepared aqua regia (3:1 HCl/HNO3) and rinsed thoroughly in pure water prior to use. The present strategy to prepare noble metal nanoclusters is very simple. In brief, 25mg CS was dissovled in 2.5mL pure water to prepare a fresh 10mg/mL solution, and then 300μL, 15mM HAuCl4 solution was added to the CS solution under vigorous stirring, after 30min later, 240μL, 20mM dopamine (DA)was added, and the reaction was completed in 5h, as confirmed by time-course measurement of the fluorescence intensity evolution (Fig. 1 -D). The color of the solution changed from colorless to light brown. Finally, the reaction solution was then passed through a filter having a cut-off molecular weight of 3kDa. The purified solution was stored at 2–8°C in the fridge for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of CS-directed Au clusters
ce:section.ce:para:
Photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield of the CS-directed Au cluster was obtained by using the comparative method of Williams et al. [39]. The quantum yield of CS-directed Au clusters, QNC, is calculated according to the following equation:
(1) QNC =QST ×(AST/ANC)×(FNC/FST)×(nNC/nST)2
Where QST is the quantum yield of the standard, A is the absorbance of the solution, F is the corrected emission intensity and n is the average refractive index of the solution. Subscripts ST and NC refer to the reference and CS-protected Au clusters, respectively. Fluorescein is used as the standard for the quantum yield correction. On the basis of the above Eq. (1), the fluorescence quantum yield of CS-directed Au clusters was carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The study of stability of CS-directed gold nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: In order to apply the CS-AuNCs for numerous applications such as biosensing and bioimaging, we could investigate the stability of CS-AuNCs prior to apply. The factors of stability including temperature, pH, storage time and salt concentration, etc. So we measured the effect of solution pH, temperature, salt concentration and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The linear curves of the fluorescence intensity of CS-protected Au nanoclusters vs. Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In this assay, copper dichloride (CuCl2) was chosen as the Cu2+ source. A CuCl2 stock solution (5μM) was prepared, and then various concentration (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.8, 2.5μM) was obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. For sensing the Cu2+, the reaction solution contained 80μL CS-directed gold nanoclusters and 100μL, pH 7.4 PBS buffer, and was kept at 30°C in a water bath for 2min; then, 20μL of various concentration by dilution of the stock solution was added to the reaction solution at 30°C and reacting for 20min. The change of fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was recorded using the F-4500 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of Cu2+ detection by CS-stabilized gold nanocluters
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the specificity of CS-AuNCs for sensing Cu2+, the reaction solution contained 80μ-L CS-AuNCs and 100μL, pH 7.4 PBS buffer, and was kept at 30°C in a water bath for 2min; then, 20μL of different mental ions and anions, such as Cu2+, K+, Ag+, Na+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −,SO4 2−, EDTA2−, and so on, were added to the reaction solution at 30°C and reacting for 20min. The change of fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was recorded using the F-4500 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: For Cu2+ sensing by CS-protected gold nanoclusters in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real samples including lake water and tap water, were collected from the lake of Taozi and laboratory of college of chemistry and chemical engineering in Hunan Normal University, respectively. The obtained samples were filtered with 0.22μM microporous membrane. Then the filtrate was used for sensing of Cu2+ according to the general procedure without additional pretreatment. For sensing Cu2+ in real samples, the reaction solution contained 80μL CS-directed gold nanoclusters and 100μL, pH 7.4 PBS buffer, and was kept at 30°C in a water bath for 2min; then, 20μL of 100-fold diluted lake water or tap water sample was added to the reaction solution at 30°C and reacting for 20min. The change of fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was recorded using the F-4500 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the synthetic conditions for CS-capped Au clusters
ce:section.ce:para: In order to seek best conditions for preparing size control of AuNCs, some parameters could be investigated, i.e., the usage of reducing agent, the molar concentration ratio of CS and gold precursors (HAuCl4∙4H2O), reaction temperature and reaction time.

On the basis of the above parameters, firstly, we considered the influence of reducing ability on the formation of CS-directed Au clusters, three commercially available reductants, i.e., ascorbic acid (AA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and dopamine (DA), were selected. Control experiments of Au clusters preparation were carried out, when AA and sodium borohydride was added into the reaction solutions, the color of the reaction solution was immediately changed from colorless to dark red, possibly due to the formation of large gold nanoparticle (GNPs). While the reaction solution with upon addition of DA, the color of the reaction solution slowly changed from colorless to light brown, because the gold precursors underwent progressive reduction to successfully form the CS-directed Au clusters. In the view of the photograph of fluorescence intensity (Figs. 1-A and S1), the emission intensity peak was not existed (the reaction solution with upon addition AA and NaBH4) in the range between 480nm and 650nm, while the reaction solution with upon addition of DA had a strong fluorescence intensity peak in this range of this wavelength band. When no reductant and only CS, there were also no emission intensity in the range between 480nm and 650nm. Based on the above situations, DA was chosen in our experiments.

Moreover, the molar ratio of concentration of CS and HAuCl4 was also important. At a fixed concentration of HAuCl4 (15mM), a certain amount of CS concentration was highly needed for the effective protection of gold nanoclusters. As could be seen from Fig. 1-B, the fluorescence intensity of CS-directed Au clusters was more highly strong with upon addition of the concentration of CS (10mg/mL). While decreasing the CS concentration to 4mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of Au clusters was very weak, possibly due to the formation of large nanoparticles without the sufficient protection by CS, while increasing the CS concentration to 12mg/mL, the photoluminescence (PL) of Au clusters was slightly decreased compared with the strongest fluorescence intensity (the usage of CS concentration was 10mg/mL). So, the molar ratio of concentration of CS and Au precursors (10mg/mL: 15mM) was chosen.

In addition, we also investigated the reaction temperature and reaction time. From Fig. 1-C, the reaction temperature was not extremely affected the PL intensity of CS-directed Au clusters in the range between 20 and 40°C. Considering the cost and simple construction, therefore, we selected room temperature as the reaction temperature. The reaction time could be expressed by time-course measurement of the fluorescence evolution, the result indicated the synthetic program was completed in 5h (Fig. 1-D). Upon the optimization of synthetic conditions, we could prepare the highly fluorescent CS-directed Au clusters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The spectra and structural characterization of as-prepared CS-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 represented the forming process of CS-AuNCs. CS was a negative polysaccharide, which contained lots of carboxyl and sulfonic group could form coordination bond with gold ions, the entrapped gold ions underwent progressive reduction to generate CS-AuNCs with upon addition of DA. The as-synthesized fluorescent AuNCs in the insert of Fig. 2 -A exhibited light brown under visible light (left), whereas they emitted green fluorescence under UV light with 365nm wavelength(right). As shown in Fig. 2-A, from optical absorption of as-synthesized CS-AuNCs, it could be concluded that no apparent surface plasmon resonance absorption peak in the range between 400 and 600nm could be observed to indicate that no big nanoparticles were generated, in other words, the CS-AuNCs were formed, while the emission spectrum showed a peak centered at 520nm with excitation band at 450nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the Au clusters were ca 4.78%, calculated with fluorescein as a reference with 440nm excitation wavelength. Referred to previous report, the fluorescence emission intensity was attributed to quantum effect of the metal atom clusters [40].

In order to better interpret the formation of as-prepared CS-Au clusters, a number of surface analysis data were needed, containing high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphology of the CS-capped AuNCs was obtained by HRTEM represented in Fig. 2-C, the as-prepared fluorescent AuNCs were mostly spherical dots with high monodispersity and the average size of CS-AuNCs was ca. 2nm. As shown in Figs. 2-B and S2, the oxidation state of the CS-AuNCs was determined by XPS, the Au 4f XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into two distinct components: 85.14eV (Au 4f7/2) and 88.75eV (Au 4f5/2), typical of Au+, and 84.0eV (Au 4f7/2) and 87.62eV (Au 4f5/2), typical of Au0. The Au0 could be arisen from the core of the CS-AuNCs via Au-Au interaction, while the Au+ could be assigned to the Au atoms at the surface of Au clusters bound with protected template (i.e., CS). The above results derived from the HRTEM image and XPS spectra indicated the successful synthesis of highly fluorescent CS-Au clusters.

To confirm the formation of CS protected Au clusters, i.e., discriminating between intrinsic fluorescence of CS and Au clusters, some experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. S3-A, by comparing the fluorescence emission intensity of CS and CS-AuNCs in the range between 480nm and 650nm, the fluorescence of CS was very weak (no peak intensity) compared to CS-AuNCs. We also investigated the PL intensity of gold precursors with DA and intrinsic fluorescence intensity of DA, from Fig. S3-A, the PL intensity was similar to CS, which was almost not peak intensity in the range between 480nm and 650nm. So we could conclude that the fluorescence of CS-AuNCs arose from Au clusters, that was to say, the CS-stabilized Au clusters were formed.

Interestingly, the CS-directed Au clusters could emit by a wide range of excitation wavelength, as described in Fig. S3-B, obvious fluorescence intensity could be observed upon the excitation wavelength band between 400nm and 460nm, and the maximum emission intensity occurred at an excitation wavelength of 450nm. In Fig. S3-B, we could see that all the maximum emission peak position localized at ca. 520nm. By virture of this situation, we could conclude that the as-prepared Au clusters using CS as the template via template-directed method were homogeneous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability investigation of CS-encapsulated Au clusters
ce:section.ce:para: In recent, many scientists devoted to explore new nanomaterials and apply for fundamental studies especially some fields existing many unsolved and complicated problems. For example, some novel nanomaterials were used for bioimaging in vivo, we should investigate the feasibility of the novel nanomaterials prior to bioimaging, e.g., biocompatibility, cytotoxicity, etc. Similarly, in order to apply for biological systems using the as-synthesized CS-directed Au nanoclusters, we also investigated the stability of CS-directed Au nanoclusters, containing some parameters, i.e., pH of the solution, temperature, storage time and salt concentration. As depicted in Fig. S4, the results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was not obviously affected by solution temperature, storage time, as well as solution salt concentration. While the solution pH (Fig. S4-D) could seriously affect fluorescence intensity change of the nanometre sized CS-AuNCs, possibly due to take off the acetyl group to degrade into simple sugars or smaller molecular weight of polysaccharides especially in low pH solution, leading to microenvironment change around the Au clusters. Considering our new nanomaterial mainly for biological systems, physiological environment (pH ca. 7.4) could not affect fluorescence of the nanometre sized CS-AuNCs. Based on the above situations, the as-prepared nanometre sized CS-AuNCs could be used for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ sensing using CS-AuNCs as fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, many nanomaterials was applied for heavy metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+) detection, including gold nanoparticles [41], noble metal nanoclusters [4], carbon nanodots/nanotube [42,43], quantum dots [44,45], etc. Among them, noble metal nanoclusters displaying bright fluorescence, ultrafine size, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility had attracted wide research interest in the past decades, so noble metal nanoclusters were frequently used to sense heavy metal ions. The mechanism of heavy metal ions detection using noble metal nanoclusters could be approximately expressed as follow: the non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic interaction [2]; etching or binding of metal core [46]; analyte/ligand binding-induced aggregation [4]; analyte/ligand binding-induced charge transfer [47]; analyte/ligand binding-induced energy transfer [31]. In this study, we used a novel macromolecules termed CS as the template to synthesize fluorescent gold nanoclusters for sensing Cu2+. Scheme 1 illustrated the detection strategy of Cu2+ by CS-AuNCs fluorescent probe. CS, a negtive polysaccharide, containing hemiacetal group, could be reduced Cu2+ to Cu+. In the Cu 2p of XPS spectrum of Fig. S5, the absence of a satellite peak at 942eV, which implied that Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ by CS molecules agreed with the sensing mechanism. Subsequently, the Cu+ could react with Au+ at the surface of the metal clusters via the non-covalent d10-d10 metallophilic interaction, as reported previously [48–50]. As shown in Fig. S6, after adding Cu2+ to the CS-AuNCs, the strong fluorescence intensity was noticeably quenched. Whereas, (ethylenedinitrilo) tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), an efficient chelating reagent for certain divalent metal ions (e.g., Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+) was mixed with Cu2+ prior to assay, the fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was hardly influenced. The result indicated that no extra Cu2+ coordinated with CS in the presence of EDTA, forming Cu+(d10)-Au+(d10) metallophilic bond, so the quenched fluorescence intenstiy of CS-AuNCs was not obviously observed.

To examine the specificity of the novel fluorescence probe for the detection of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence response of the CS-stabilized gold nanoclusters taward a serials of metal ions and anions including K+, Ag+, Na+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −,SO4 2−, EDTA2− was evaluated under the same conditions. From Fig. 3 -A, it could be seen that only Cu2+ caused obvious fluorescence intensity quenching at physiological pH, even Cu2+ ions coexisting with other metal ions, the results agreed with the above situation (Fig. S7). At this pH, the anions could not influence the fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs (Fig. 3-B). In brief, the high specificity of the flurescent probe indicated that they could be used to detect Cu2+ in complex systems. We could conclude that the CS molecules at the surface of AuNCs for sensing Cu2+ played at least two important roles: (1) to generate coordinates with Cu2+ and reduce Cu2+ to Cu+, resulting in fluorescence intensity quenching of fluorecent gold nanoclusters via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophillic bond, and (2) to prevent other small molecules, metal ions and anions from approaching the surface of Au nanoclusters.

As illustrated in Fig. S8, addition of Cu2+ to the aqueous solution of CS-AuNCs leaded to dramatic fluorescence quenching of CS-AuNCs. Temporal change of the quenched fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs in the absence or presence of 2.5μM Cu2+ was monitored for 30min, the maximal and stable quenched fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs was obtained after approximately 20min interaction. In Fig. 4 , under the optimal contiditions, with the increase in the added concentration of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of CS-mediated gold nanoclusters could be decreased. A linear reationship between the decreased fluorescence intenstiy of CS-AuNCs and the concentration of added Cu2+ was obtained over the range from 0.1 to 1.8μM, and the corresponding linear function was y=−1.0671x+2323.9 with a correlation coefficient of R2 =0.997. The limit of detection (LOD) was 8nM (S/N=3), which was relatively lower than that of previous reports, and detailed comparison was shown in Table S1.

To validate the practicality of the as-prepared CS-AuNCs fluorescent probe, we used the highly selective and sensitive proposed strategy based on Cu2+-mediated the fluorescence intensity diminishing for the determination of Cu2+ in real samples. The detection results of Cu2+ in lake water and tap water via CS-AuNCs were listed in Table 1 . The recoveries of the spiked samples by the proposed method ranged from 90% to 108%. The results implied that the as-synthesized CS-AuNCs fluorescent probe could be used to sense Cu2+ selectively and sensitively in real water samples.

In conclusion, a convenient, green and cost-effective protocol for the preparation of stable, water-soluble, high fluorescence emissive gold clusters was proposed by using commercially available polysaccharide (CS) as the stabilizing agent via template-based mothod. Because CS contained much of carboxyl and sulfonate groups, which could form coordinate bond with metal cations, such as gold and silver ions, and then the Au ions underwent progressive reduction to generate the CS-protected Au clusters. We also investigated the optimal conditions for preparing the highly fluorescent CS-templated Au clusters, i.e., the usage of reducing agent, the molar concentration ratio of CS and HAuCl4, reaction temperature and reaction time. In comparison with the above parameters, optimization conditions were selected for directing synthesis of Au clusters. Furthermore, the as-prepared CS-AuNCs fluorescence probe reacted under moderate condition and was photostable under high salt strength. The CS-coated AuNCs were further directly used as a label-free probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in real samples including lake water and tap water samples via non-covalent d10-d10 metallophillic bond. The CS protective layer not only entraped and reduced Cu2+ to Cu+, leading to quenched fluorescence intensity of CS-AuNCs; but also protected other interferences from touching the surface of gold nanoclusters. The satisfactory results were obtained for determination of Cu2+ using CS-AuNCs probe in lake water and tap water samples. Moreover, CS molecule had hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which could form bioadhesion on biological tissues. Based on long-term stability and multifunctional groups of CS-templated gold nanoclusters, they can be used as promising fluorescent nanoprobes for further development including bioimaging, drug delivery and cancer radiation therapy.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575040, 21405044, 21275049, 21275049), the Department of Education of Hunan Province (14C1188), and the foundation for innovative research groups of the Hunan Natural Science Foundation of China (2015JC1001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.130.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ruizhi Wen is an associate professor of the College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology. At present, she is a Ph.D. student in Analytical Chemisty under supervision of professor Bo Chen in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University.
Hongchang Li is a Ph.D. student in Analytical Chemisty under supervision of professor Bo Chen in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University.

Bo Chen is a professor of the Key Laboratory of Phytochemical R&D of Hunan Province, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University. His research interests focus on chromatography analysis; mass spectrometry; synthesis and application of nanomaterials in life science and environmental science.
Liping Wang is a professor of Hunan Analysis and Testing Center.
